title: Auto-correct Suggestion (Edit Distance Top-K)
description: Given query word q and dictionary of up to 10^5 words, return top-K closest words by edit distance. Break ties lexicographically. Example: q=aple, dict includes apple, apply, ape → top suggestions.

examples.explanation: Query: 'aple' (length 4). Dictionary: ['apple', 'apply', 'ape', 'app', 'ple']. Compute edit distances: 1. 'apple' vs 'aple': DP matrix shows distance 1 (insert 'p' at position 2). 2. 'apply' vs 'aple': distance 2 (substitute 'p'→'p', insert 'l' at position 4). 3. 'ape' vs 'aple': distance 1 (insert 'l' at position 2). 4. 'app' vs 'aple': distance 2 (substitute 'p'→'l', delete 'e'). 5. 'ple' vs 'aple': distance 2 (delete 'a', substitute 'p'→'p'). Distances: [('apple',1), ('apply',2), ('ape',1), ('app',2), ('ple',2)]. Sort by distance then lexicographically: [(1,'ape'), (1,'apple'), (2,'app'), (2,'apply'), (2,'ple')]. Top 3: ['ape', 'apple', 'apply'] but wait—tie-breaking: at distance 1, 'ape' comes before 'apple' lexicographically. So final: ['ape', 'apple', 'apply'].

Query: 'cat' (length 3). Dictionary: ['bat', 'cats', 'cut', 'dog', 'at', 'cat']. Compute distances: 1. 'bat' vs 'cat': distance 1 (substitute 'b'→'c'). 2. 'cats' vs 'cat': distance 1 (delete 's'). 3. 'cut' vs 'cat': distance 1 (substitute 'u'→'a'). 4. 'dog' vs 'cat': distance 3 (all letters different). 5. 'at' vs 'cat': distance 1 (delete 'c'). 6. 'cat' vs 'cat': distance 0 (identical). Distances: [('bat',1), ('cats',1), ('cut',1), ('dog',3), ('at',1), ('cat',0)]. Sort: [(0,'cat'), (1,'at'), (1,'bat'), (1,'cats'), (1,'cut'), (3,'dog')]. Top 2: ['cat', 'at'] but tie-breaking: at distance 1, 'at' comes before 'bat' lexicographically. So final: ['cat', 'at'].

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force with Edit Distance O(n*m)
approaches.summary: Compute edit distance (e.g., Levenshtein) between query q and every dictionary word, sort results by distance and lexicographically, then return top-K. This is straightforward but slow for large dictionaries.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(N * L_q * L_d) where N is dictionary size, L_q is query length, L_d is average dictionary word length
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing distances and words

ladders.title: Full Problem — Auto-correct Suggestion with Brute Force Edit Distance
ladders.desc: Given a query word and dictionary, return top-K closest words by edit distance with tie-breaking.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete implementation of the brute force approach for auto-correct suggestions. The intuition is straightforward: we need to find the K dictionary words that are most similar to the query word, where similarity is measured by edit distance (minimum number of insertions, deletions, or substitutions needed to transform one word into another). Since we're using brute force, we'll compute edit distance between the query and every dictionary word, then sort all results to find the top K. The algorithm works as follows: 1. Initialize an empty list to store (distance, word) pairs. 2. For each word in the dictionary, compute its edit distance from the query using dynamic programming (Levenshtein distance). 3. Append (distance, word) to our list. 4. Sort the list primarily by distance (ascending), and secondarily by word (lexicographically ascending) for tie-breaking. 5. Return the first K words from the sorted list. Time complexity is O(N * L_q * L_d) where N is dictionary size, L_q is query length, and L_d is average dictionary word length, due to computing edit distance for each word. Space complexity is O(N) for storing distances and words. This approach is conceptually simple but computationally expensive for large dictionaries, making it impractical for real-time applications but excellent for understanding the problem fundamentals. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle empty dictionaries, not implementing proper tie-breaking, using inefficient edit distance algorithms, and not considering edge cases like identical words. The connection to real applications is direct—this is how basic spell checkers work, though optimized versions use more sophisticated data structures.

examples.trace: Query: 'aple' (length 4). Dictionary: ['apple', 'apply', 'ape', 'app', 'ple']. Compute edit distances: 1. 'apple' vs 'aple': DP matrix shows distance 1 (insert 'p' at position 2). 2. 'apply' vs 'aple': distance 2 (substitute 'p'→'p', insert 'l' at position 4). 3. 'ape' vs 'aple': distance 1 (insert 'l' at position 2). 4. 'app' vs 'aple': distance 2 (substitute 'p'→'l', delete 'e'). 5. 'ple' vs 'aple': distance 2 (delete 'a', substitute 'p'→'p'). Distances: [('apple',1), ('apply',2), ('ape',1), ('app',2), ('ple',2)]. Sort by distance then lexicographically: [(1,'ape'), (1,'apple'), (2,'app'), (2,'apply'), (2,'ple')]. Top 3: ['ape', 'apple', 'apply'] but wait—tie-breaking: at distance 1, 'ape' comes before 'apple' lexicographically. So final: ['ape', 'apple', 'apply'].

Query: 'cat' (length 3). Dictionary: ['bat', 'cats', 'cut', 'dog', 'at', 'cat']. Compute distances: 1. 'bat' vs 'cat': distance 1 (substitute 'b'→'c'). 2. 'cats' vs 'cat': distance 1 (delete 's'). 3. 'cut' vs 'cat': distance 1 (substitute 'u'→'a'). 4. 'dog' vs 'cat': distance 3 (all letters different). 5. 'at' vs 'cat': distance 1 (delete 'c'). 6. 'cat' vs 'cat': distance 0 (identical). Distances: [('bat',1), ('cats',1), ('cut',1), ('dog',3), ('at',1), ('cat',0)]. Sort: [(0,'cat'), (1,'at'), (1,'bat'), (1,'cats'), (1,'cut'), (3,'dog')]. Top 2: ['cat', 'at'] but tie-breaking: at distance 1, 'at' comes before 'bat' lexicographically. So final: ['cat', 'at'].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Edit Distance Computation (Levenshtein Distance)
ladders.desc: Compute the minimum edit distance between two strings using dynamic programming.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach—calculating how many edits (insertions, deletions, substitutions) are needed to transform one string into another. The intuition is to build a DP table where dp[i][j] represents the edit distance between the first i characters of string A and first j characters of string B. We fill this table iteratively: if characters match, no extra cost; otherwise, we take the minimum of three operations (insert, delete, substitute) plus one. The algorithm: 1. Initialize a DP matrix of size (len(A)+1) x (len(B)+1) with dp[i][0] = i and dp[0][j] = j. 2. For i from 1 to len(A): For j from 1 to len(B): If A[i-1] == B[j-1], dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]. Else, dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]). 3. Return dp[len(A)][len(B)]. Time complexity is O(L_A * L_B) where L_A and L_B are string lengths. Space complexity is O(L_A * L_B) for the DP table, but can be optimized to O(min(L_A, L_B)) using two rows. This feeds into the full problem by providing the distance metric for each dictionary word. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices, misunderstanding the cost of operations, and not initializing the base cases correctly. Real code pattern uses nested loops and careful indexing.

examples.trace: Strings: 'kitten' (length 6), 'sitting' (length 7). Initialize DP table 7x8. Base: row0 = [0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7], col0 = [0,1,2,3,4,5,6]. Fill: i=1,j=1: 'k' vs 's' mismatch, dp[1][1]=1+min(dp[0][1]=1, dp[1][0]=1, dp[0][0]=0)=1. i=1,j=2: 'k' vs 'i' mismatch, dp[1][2]=1+min(2,1,1)=2. Continue... Final dp[6][7]=3. Operations: substitute 'k'→'s', substitute 'e'→'i', insert 'g'.

Strings: 'abc' (length 3), 'abc' (length 3). DP table 4x4. Base: row0=[0,1,2,3], col0=[0,1,2,3]. Fill: i=1,j=1: 'a' vs 'a' match, dp[1][1]=dp[0][0]=0. i=1,j=2: 'a' vs 'b' mismatch, dp[1][2]=1+min(1,0,1)=1. i=2,j=1: 'b' vs 'a' mismatch, dp[2][1]=1+min(0,1,1)=1. i=2,j=2: 'b' vs 'b' match, dp[2][2]=dp[1][1]=0. i=2,j=3: 'b' vs 'c' mismatch, dp[2][3]=1+min(1,0,1)=1. i=3,j=2: 'c' vs 'b' mismatch, dp[3][2]=1+min(1,1,1)=2. i=3,j=3: 'c' vs 'c' match, dp[3][3]=dp[2][2]=0. Final dp[3][3]=0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sorting with Custom Comparator for Distance and Lexicographic Order
ladders.desc: Sort a list of (distance, word) pairs primarily by distance ascending, then by word ascending.
ladders.explanation: This core logic handles the ranking of dictionary words after computing edit distances. The intuition is that we need to order words first by their similarity to the query (lower edit distance is better), and then break ties using alphabetical order to ensure deterministic results. This is implemented using a custom comparator in the sorting function. The algorithm: 1. Given a list of tuples [(distance1, word1), (distance2, word2), ...]. 2. Sort the list using a key that prioritizes distance first, then word. In Python, this can be done with sorted(list, key=lambda x: (x[0], x[1])). 3. The sorted list will have words in ascending order of distance, and within the same distance, in lexicographically ascending order. Time complexity is O(N log N) for sorting N items. Space complexity is O(N) for the list. This feeds into the full problem by organizing the computed distances for easy extraction of top K. Common mistakes include: sorting only by distance and ignoring tie-breaking, using descending order incorrectly, or not handling empty lists. Real code pattern involves the sorted() function with a tuple key.

examples.trace: Input list: [(2,'apply'), (1,'apple'), (1,'ape')]. Sort key: for each tuple (d,w), use (d,w). Compare: (1,'ape') vs (1,'apple'): distances equal (1), compare words: 'ape' < 'apple' lexicographically, so 'ape' comes first. Then (1,'apple') vs (2,'apply'): distance 1 < 2, so 'apple' before 'apply'. Sorted: [(1,'ape'), (1,'apple'), (2,'apply')].

Input: [(0,'cat'), (1,'at'), (1,'bat'), (3,'dog')]. Sort: (0,'cat') has smallest distance 0, comes first. Next, (1,'at') and (1,'bat'): distances equal 1, compare words: 'at' < 'bat', so 'at' before 'bat'. Then (3,'dog') last. Sorted: [(0,'cat'), (1,'at'), (1,'bat'), (3,'dog')].

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Over a Dictionary and Storing Results
ladders.desc: Loop through dictionary words, compute a value for each, and collect results in a list.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves processing each word in a dictionary to perform some operation (like computing edit distance) and accumulating the results. The intuition is to systematically handle each element in a collection, which is fundamental in many algorithms. The algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty list 'results'. 2. For each word in the dictionary: a. Compute some value based on the word (e.g., edit distance from a query). b. Append a tuple (value, word) to 'results'. 3. Return the 'results' list. Time complexity is O(N * T) where N is dictionary size and T is time per word operation. Space complexity is O(N) for storing results. This feeds into the core logic by providing the list to be sorted. Common mistakes include: modifying the dictionary while iterating, not handling empty dictionaries, or inefficient appending. Real code pattern uses a for-loop over the dictionary list.

examples.trace: Dictionary: ['apple', 'ape']. Query: 'aple'. Initialize results=[]. For word='apple': compute edit distance('apple','aple')=1, append (1,'apple') to results. For word='ape': compute edit distance('ape','aple')=1, append (1,'ape') to results. Final results=[(1,'apple'), (1,'ape')].
Dictionary: ['cat', 'dog']. Query: 'cat'. Results=[]. Word='cat': distance=0, append (0,'cat'). Word='dog': distance=3, append (3,'dog'). Results=[(0,'cat'), (3,'dog')].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Length and Character Access
ladders.desc: Get the length of a string and access individual characters by index.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves fundamental string manipulations: determining how many characters are in a string and retrieving a specific character at a given position. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we often need to know their size or examine particular elements, which is essential for algorithms like edit distance. The algorithm: 1. For length: use len(string) to return the number of characters. 2. For character access: use string[index] to get the character at position index (0-based). Time complexity is O(1) for both operations in most programming languages. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the building block by providing the necessary data for distance computations. Common mistakes include: using 1-based indexing, accessing out-of-bounds indices, or confusing length with other properties. Real code pattern uses simple function calls or indexing.

examples.trace: String: 'hello'. Length: len('hello') counts characters: 'h','e','l','l','o' → 5. Character at index 1: string[1] returns the second character (since indexing starts at 0), which is 'e'.
String: 'a'. Length: len('a')=1. Character at index 0: string[0]='a'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Strings as Sequences
ladders.desc: Recognize that strings are ordered collections of characters.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces strings as fundamental data structures: they are sequences of characters, meaning they have an order (first character, second character, etc.) and a length. The intuition is that in programming, we often treat text as a string, which allows us to manipulate it character by character. For example, the string 'cat' consists of characters 'c', 'a', 't' in that order. This concept is prerequisite for operations like accessing characters or computing edit distance, which rely on comparing sequences. There's no complex algorithm here—just the understanding that strings can be indexed and iterated. Time and space analysis are not applicable at this basic level. This feeds into basic operations by providing the conceptual basis for length and access. Common mistakes include: thinking of strings as unchangeable without proper methods, or confusing strings with lists. Real code pattern involves declaring strings with quotes.

examples.trace: String: 'dog'. It is a sequence of 3 characters: position 0: 'd', position 1: 'o', position 2: 'g'. We can think of it as a list: ['d','o','g'].
String: 'ab'. Sequence: character 0 is 'a', character 1 is 'b'. Length is 2.

approaches.name: Approach 3: BK-Tree with Edit Distance O(log N * L_q * L_d)
approaches.summary: Construct a BK-tree from the dictionary using edit distance as the metric, then query the tree to find top-K closest words by performing a bounded search. This organizes words hierarchically to reduce comparisons.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(log N * L_q * L_d) on average for querying, with O(N * L_d) for preprocessing

ladders.title: Full Problem — BK-Tree Top-K Edit Distance Suggestions
ladders.desc: Complete solution using BK-tree to find top-K closest dictionary words to a query by edit distance with tie-breaking.
ladders.explanation: This solution implements the full auto-correct suggestion system using BK-tree optimization. The intuition is that edit distance satisfies the triangle inequality, allowing us to organize words in a metric tree where each node's children are within specific edit distance ranges from their parent. This hierarchical structure dramatically reduces comparisons during queries. The algorithm has two phases: preprocessing where we build the BK-tree from the dictionary in O(N*L_d) time, and querying where we perform a bounded depth-first search to find top-K candidates. For querying, we start at the root and recursively explore children whose distance ranges intersect with [d-K, d+K] where d is edit distance from query to current node. We maintain a max-heap of size K to track best candidates, using edit distance as primary key and lexicographic order as tie-breaker. The search bound K ensures we don't explore unnecessary branches. Time complexity is O(log N * L_q * L_d) on average due to tree height being logarithmic, though worst-case could be O(N) for unbalanced trees. Space is O(N) for tree storage plus O(K) for the heap. Connection to L1: This integrates the BK-tree query subroutine with heap management for top-K selection. Common mistakes include incorrect tie-breaking (must be lexicographic after edit distance), forgetting to handle empty dictionaries, and not properly bounding the search (which causes O(N) worst-case). Real code pattern: class BKNode with word and children dict mapping distances to child nodes; build_tree() inserting each word; query() with recursive bounded DFS and heap management.

examples.trace: 1. Preprocessing: Build BK-tree with root='apple'. Insert 'apply': dist(apple,apply)=2 → child at distance 2. Insert 'ape': dist(apple,ape)=2 → conflict at distance 2, recursively insert under 'apply' with dist(apply,ape)=1 → child of 'apply' at distance 1. Insert 'app': dist(apple,app)=1 → child at distance 1. Insert 'ample': dist(apple,ample)=2 → conflict at distance 2, recursively under 'apply' with dist(apply,ample)=3 → child of 'apply' at distance 3. Insert 'ale': dist(apple,ale)=3 → child at distance 3. Insert 'plea': dist(apple,plea)=4 → child at distance 4. 2. Query q='aple' with K=3: Start at root 'apple' with dist=1. Heap: [('apple',1)]. Explore children: distance 1 child 'app' with dist(aple,app)=2 → heap: [('apple',1),('app',2),('apply',2)] after exploring 'apply' subtree. Distance 2 child 'apply': dist(aple,apply)=2 → explore its children: distance 1 child 'ape' with dist(aple,ape)=1 → heap becomes [('ape',1),('apple',1),('apply',2)]. Distance 3 child 'ale': dist(aple,ale)=2 → heap unchanged (worse than current). Distance 4 child 'plea': dist=3 → not better. Final heap top-K sorted: ['ape','apple','apply'] → after tie-breaking lexicographically for same distances: 'apple' (dist1) then 'ape' (dist1) then 'apply' (dist2).

1. Build BK-tree: root='bat'. Insert 'car': dist(bat,car)=2 → child at distance 2. Insert 'cart': dist(bat,cart)=3 → child at distance 3. Insert 'cut': dist(bat,cut)=2 → conflict at distance 2, recursively under 'car' with dist(car,cut)=1 → child of 'car' at distance 1. Insert 'cats': dist(bat,cats)=3 → conflict at distance 3, recursively under 'cart' with dist(cart,cats)=1 → child of 'cart' at distance 1. Insert 'dog': dist(bat,dog)=3 → conflict at distance 3, recursively under 'cart' with dist(cart,dog)=3 → child of 'cart' at distance 3. Insert 'can': dist(bat,can)=2 → conflict at distance 2, recursively under 'car' with dist(car,can)=1 → conflict at distance 1 under 'car', recursively under 'cut' with dist(cut,can)=2 → child of 'cut' at distance 2. 2. Query q='cat': Start at root 'bat' with dist=1. Heap: [('bat',1)]. Explore children: distance 2 child 'car': dist(cat,car)=1 → heap: [('car',1),('bat',1)]. Explore 'car' subtree: its child at distance 1 'cut' with dist(cat,cut)=1 → heap unchanged (same distance, lexicographically 'car' < 'cut'). Distance 3 child 'cart': dist(cat,cart)=1 → heap unchanged ('car' and 'bat' have distance 1, 'cart' distance 1 but lexicographically 'car' < 'cart'). Final top-2: ['bat','car'] (both distance 1, sorted lexicographically).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BK-Tree Query with Bounded Search
ladders.desc: Query a BK-tree to find all words within edit distance bound from a query word.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine performs the core query operation on a BK-tree. The intuition leverages the triangle inequality property of edit distance: if the distance from query Q to node N is d, then any word in the subtree rooted at child C (where child is at distance δ from N) must have distance to Q in the range [|d-δ|, d+δ]. This allows us to prune branches where this range doesn't intersect with our interest region. The algorithm performs a depth-first search starting from the root: 1. Compute edit distance d between Q and current node word. 2. Add current word to results if within bound. 3. For each child at distance δ from current node, if |d-δ| ≤ bound, recursively search that child subtree. The bound parameter controls search radius. Time complexity is O(log N * L_q * L_d) on average as we explore only relevant branches, but worst-case O(N) if tree is unbalanced. Space is O(N) for recursion stack. Connection to L0: This subroutine finds candidates within bound, which L0 then filters to top-K using a heap. Common mistakes include incorrect range calculation (must use absolute value), not handling multiple children at same distance (BK-tree allows only one child per distance value), and infinite recursion if tree has cycles (shouldn't happen in proper implementation). Real code pattern: def query(node, q, bound, results): d = edit_distance(q, node.word); if d <= bound: results.append((node.word, d)); for dist, child in node.children.items(): if abs(d-dist) <= bound: query(child, q, bound, results).

examples.input: BK-tree root='apple' with children: dist2→'apply', dist1→'app', dist3→'ale', dist4→'plea'; 'apply' has child dist1→'ape'; query q='aple', bound=2
examples.trace: Start at root 'apple': dist(aple,apple)=1 ≤2 → add 'apple'. Check children: dist1 child 'app': |1-1|=0 ≤2 → explore. dist2 child 'apply': |1-2|=1 ≤2 → explore. dist3 child 'ale': |1-3|=2 ≤2 → explore. dist4 child 'plea': |1-4|=3 >2 → prune. Recursively: Node 'app': dist(aple,app)=2 ≤2 → add 'app'. No children. Node 'apply': dist(aple,apply)=2 ≤2 → add 'apply'. Check its child: dist1 child 'ape': |2-1|=1 ≤2 → explore. Node 'ape': dist(aple,ape)=1 ≤2 → add 'ape'. Node 'ale': dist(aple,ale)=2 ≤2 → add 'ale'. Final results: ['apple','app','apply','ape','ale'] but note 'ale' distance=2 included.

examples.input: BK-tree root='bat' with children: dist2→'car', dist3→'cart'; 'car' has child dist1→'cut'; query q='cat', bound=1
examples.trace: Start at root 'bat': dist(cat,bat)=1 ≤1 → add 'bat'. Check children: dist2 child 'car': |1-2|=1 ≤1 → explore. dist3 child 'cart': |1-3|=2 >1 → prune. Node 'car': dist(cat,car)=1 ≤1 → add 'car'. Check its child: dist1 child 'cut': |1-1|=0 ≤1 → explore. Node 'cut': dist(cat,cut)=1 ≤1 → add 'cut'. But wait, 'cut' distance=1, so output should include it. Let's correct: Actually dist(cat,cut)=2 (c-a→c-u, t→t, so 2 substitutions? Let's compute properly: 'cat' to 'cut': c→c, a→u (1), t→t, so edit distance=1. Yes, so 'cut' should be included. Final results: ['bat','car','cut'].

testCases.input: root='test', children: dist1→'best', dist2→'rest', dist3→'nest'; query q='west', bound=1
root='hello', children: dist1→'hell', dist2→'hallo', dist3→'jello'; 'hell' has child dist1→'hel'; query q='helo', bound=2
root='a', children: dist1→'aa', dist2→'aaa'; 'aa' has child dist1→'aaa'; query q='a', bound=0
root='book', children: dist1→'cook', dist2→'hook', dist3→'look'; query q='took', bound=1
root='long', children: dist2→'short', dist3→'word'; query q='longword', bound=5
root='similar', children: dist1→'similer', dist2→'similiar'; query q='similiar', bound=1
root='qwerty', children: none; query q='qwerty', bound=0
root='abc', children: dist3→'def', dist6→'ghi'; query q='xyz', bound=2
root='apple', children: dist2→'apply', dist3→'ape'; query q='aple', bound=3
root='cat', children: dist1→'bat', dist2→'car', dist3→'cut'; query q='dog', bound=10

ladders.title: Core Logic — BK-Tree Construction Using Edit Distance Metric
ladders.desc: Build a BK-tree by inserting words using edit distance as the metric space distance function.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on constructing the BK-tree, which is the data structure enabling efficient queries. The intuition is that edit distance forms a metric space (non-negative, symmetric, triangle inequality), allowing us to build a tree where each node's children are organized by their edit distance from the parent. When inserting a new word, we start at the root and compute edit distance d to current node. If the current node already has a child at distance d, we recursively insert under that child; otherwise, we create a new child at that distance. This creates a hierarchical clustering where similar words are grouped together. The algorithm: 1. Start with first dictionary word as root. 2. For each subsequent word w: insert(root, w). Insert function: compute d = edit_distance(node.word, w). If d == 0, word already exists (handle as needed). Else if node has child at distance d, recursively insert(child, w). Else create new child node with word w at distance d. Time complexity is O(N*L_d) for N insertions each taking O(L_d) for edit distance, and tree height affects recursion but typically O(log N). Space is O(N) for nodes. Connection to L1: The constructed tree is what gets queried. Common mistakes include not handling duplicate words (should ignore or count), assuming edit distance is commutative (it is), and creating multiple children at same distance (BK-tree allows only one per distance). Real code pattern: class BKNode: def __init__(self, word): self.word = word; self.children = {}. def insert(node, word): d = edit_distance(node.word, word); if d == 0: return; if d in node.children: insert(node.children[d], word); else: node.children[d] = BKNode(word).

examples.output: Tree: root='apple' with child at distance 2→'apply', and 'apply' has child at distance 1→'ape'
examples.trace: 1. Root = 'apple'. 2. Insert 'apply': dist(apple,apply)=2. Root has no child at distance 2 → create child 'apply' at distance 2. 3. Insert 'ape': dist(apple,ape)=2. Root has child at distance 2 ('apply') → recursively insert under 'apply'. dist(apply,ape)=1. 'apply' has no child at distance 1 → create child 'ape' at distance 1 under 'apply'. Final tree: apple --dist2--> apply --dist1--> ape.

examples.output: Tree: root='bat' with child at distance 2→'car', 'car' has child at distance 1→'cut', 'cut' has child at distance 2→'can'
examples.trace: 1. Root = 'bat'. 2. Insert 'car': dist(bat,car)=2 → create child 'car' at distance 2. 3. Insert 'cut': dist(bat,cut)=2 → conflict at distance 2, recursively under 'car'. dist(car,cut)=1 → 'car' has no child at distance 1 → create child 'cut' at distance 1 under 'car'. 4. Insert 'can': dist(bat,can)=2 → conflict at distance 2, recursively under 'car'. dist(car,can)=1 → conflict at distance 1 under 'car', recursively under 'cut'. dist(cut,can)=2 → 'cut' has no child at distance 2 → create child 'can' at distance 2 under 'cut'.

testCases.expected: root='test' with child dist1->'best'; 'best' has child dist1->'rest'
root='book' with child dist1->'cook'; 'cook' has child dist1->'hook'; 'hook' has child dist1->'look'
root='long' with children: dist4->'short' (dist long-short=4), dist3->'word' (dist long-word=3)
root='similar' with child dist1->'similer'; 'similer' has child dist2->'similiar'
root='cat' with child dist1->'bat'; 'bat' has child dist2->'car'

ladders.title: Building Block — Edit Distance (Levenshtein Distance) Computation
ladders.desc: Compute the minimum number of single-character edits (insert, delete, substitute) to transform one string into another.
ladders.explanation: Edit distance, also known as Levenshtein distance, is the fundamental metric used in BK-tree. The intuition is dynamic programming: we build a 2D table dp where dp[i][j] represents edit distance between first i characters of str1 and first j characters of str2. The recurrence relation: dp[i][j] = 0 if i==0 and j==0; j if i==0; i if j==0; otherwise min(dp[i-1][j]+1 (delete), dp[i][j-1]+1 (insert), dp[i-1][j-1] + (0 if str1[i-1]==str2[j-1] else 1) (substitute/match)). This captures all possible edit sequences. The algorithm: 1. Initialize dp array of size (len1+1) x (len2+1). 2. Fill first row and column with incremental values (base cases). 3. For i from 1 to len1, j from 1 to len2: compute dp[i][j] using recurrence. 4. Return dp[len1][len2]. Time complexity is O(L1*L2) where L are string lengths. Space can be optimized to O(min(L1,L2)) by keeping only two rows. Connection to L2: This distance function is used during BK-tree insertion and query. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices, incorrect base cases, and not considering all three operations. Real code pattern: def edit_distance(s1, s2): m,n=len(s1),len(s2); dp=[[0]*(n+1) for _ in range(m+1)]; for i in range(m+1): dp[i][0]=i; for j in range(n+1): dp[0][j]=j; for i in range(1,m+1): for j in range(1,n+1): cost=0 if s1[i-1]==s2[j-1] else 1; dp[i][j]=min(dp[i-1][j]+1, dp[i][j-1]+1, dp[i-1][j-1]+cost); return dp[m][n].

examples.trace: DP table size 7x8. Initialize first row: [0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7]; first column: [0,1,2,3,4,5,6]. Compute: i=1,j=1: k vs s, cost=1, min(dp[0][1]+1=2, dp[1][0]+1=2, dp[0][0]+1=1) → 1. i=1,j=2: k vs i, cost=1, min(dp[0][2]+1=3, dp[1][1]+1=2, dp[0][1]+1=2) → 2. Continue... Final dp[6][7]=3. Steps: kitten → sitten (substitute k→s), sitten → sittin (substitute e→i), sittin → sitting (insert g).

DP table 6x5. Initialize: row0: [0,1,2,3,4]; col0: [0,1,2,3,4,5]. Compute: i=1,j=1: a vs a, cost=0, min(2,2,0)=0. i=2,j=2: p vs p, cost=0, min(1,1,0)=0. i=3,j=3: p vs l, cost=1, min(1,1,1)=1. i=4,j=3: l vs l, cost=0, min(2,2,1)=1. i=5,j=4: e vs e, cost=0, min(2,2,1)=1. dp[5][4]=1. Transformation: apple → aple (delete one p).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Comparison and Character Operations
ladders.desc: Compare strings character by character and perform basic string manipulations.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level involves working with strings at the character level, which is essential for edit distance computation. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we need to access individual characters, compare them, and understand string properties like length. Key operations include: 1. Accessing character at index i using s[i]. 2. Comparing two characters for equality. 3. Iterating through strings with loops. 4. Understanding string immutability (in many languages). 5. Basic manipulations like concatenation, slicing, or building new strings. For edit distance, we primarily need character access and comparison. The algorithm for a simple string equality check would be: if len(s1) != len(s2): return False; for i in range(len(s1)): if s1[i] != s2[i]: return False; return True. Time complexity O(L) for length L. Space O(1). Connection to L3: These operations are used in the inner loop of edit distance DP when comparing characters. Common mistakes include index out of bounds, forgetting that strings are 0-indexed, and confusion between characters and strings (e.g., 'a' vs 'a'). Real code pattern: In Python, strings are immutable; s='hello'; char=s[0] gives 'h'; len(s) gives 5; for ch in s: iterates characters.

examples.trace: Index 0: 'a' == 'a'. Index 1: 'p' == 'p'. Index 2: 'p' == 'p'. Index 3: 'l' == 'l'. Index 4: 'e' != 'y'. So strings differ at position 4.

examples.input: Build string from characters: take 'cat' and change 'a' to 'u'
examples.trace: Start with 'cat'. Characters: c, a, t. We want to change second character from 'a' to 'u'. Since strings are immutable, we create new string: first character 'c', then 'u', then 't'. Concatenate: 'c' + 'u' + 't' = 'cut'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Strings as Sequences and Basic Loops
ladders.desc: Understand strings as ordered collections of characters and use loops to traverse them.
ladders.explanation: This is the most basic prerequisite: understanding what a string is and how to process it element by element. A string is a sequence of characters, like 'hello' consists of characters 'h','e','l','l','o'. In programming, we often use loops to go through each character one by one. The intuition is similar to counting or listing items: we start at the beginning and move to the end, performing some operation on each character. For example, to print each character: for character in my_string: print(character). This uses a for-loop, which automatically iterates over the sequence. Alternatively, we can use while-loops with an index variable. Key concepts: 1. Strings are ordered (first character, second, etc.). 2. We can access length with len(string). 3. Loops repeat a block of code for each element. 4. Indexing starts at 0 in most languages. Time complexity for traversing a string is O(L) where L is length. Space is O(1) if we just access characters. Connection to L4: This foundational skill enables character comparisons and manipulations. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., trying to access string[length] which is out of bounds), infinite loops, and confusion between characters and strings. Real code pattern: In Python, s = 'cat'; for i in range(len(s)): print(s[i]) prints c, a, t.

examples.input: Traverse string 'abc' and print each character
examples.trace: Initialize loop: first iteration, character = 'a', print 'a'. Second iteration, character = 'b', print 'b'. Third iteration, character = 'c', print 'c'. Loop ends.
Use len('hello'): counts characters: h,e,l,l,o → 5 characters. Alternatively, manual count: start count=0, for each character in 'hello': increment count. After loop: count=5.

testCases.input: What is the first character of 'banana'?
What is the last character of 'zebra'?

testCases.expected: Yes, it's a string of length 1

testCases.input: Loop over '123' and sum digits (as integers)
Print each character of 'dog' on separate lines

approaches.name: Approach 4: Locality-Sensitive Hashing with Approximate Edit Distance O(N * L)
approaches.summary: Use LSH techniques (e.g., based on n-grams or embeddings) to hash words into buckets, then compute exact edit distances only within candidate buckets to approximate top-K results. This trades accuracy for speed in large-scale scenarios.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(N * L) for preprocessing and O(1) on average for querying with approximation
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(N) for hash tables and buckets

ladders.title: Full Problem — Locality-Sensitive Hashing for Edit Distance Top-K
ladders.desc: Complete implementation using LSH with n-grams to find top-K closest dictionary words to a query word by edit distance.
ladders.explanation: This solution addresses the problem of finding the top-K closest dictionary words to a query word by edit distance efficiently for large dictionaries (up to 10^5 words). The core challenge is that computing edit distance between the query and every dictionary word is O(N*L^2) which is too slow. We use Locality-Sensitive Hashing (LSH) with n-grams to approximate the solution. The intuition is that words with small edit distances will share many n-grams, so we can hash words based on their n-gram sets into buckets, then only compute exact edit distances for words in the same buckets as the query. This trades perfect accuracy for significant speed improvements. The algorithm has three phases: 1) Preprocessing: For each dictionary word, generate its n-grams (substrings of length n), create multiple hash functions by randomly selecting subsets of n-grams, hash the word to multiple buckets, and store word references in those buckets. 2) Querying: For the query word, generate the same n-grams, apply the same hash functions to get bucket IDs, collect all candidate words from those buckets (with deduplication), compute exact edit distances for all candidates, sort by (distance, word) to get top-K. 3) Optimization: We use multiple hash functions (bands) to increase recall - words need to match in at least one band to be considered candidates. The time complexity is O(N*L) for preprocessing (generating n-grams for all words) and O(1) on average for querying (since we only examine a small fraction of the dictionary). Space is O(N) for storing the hash tables. The approximation comes from potentially missing some relevant words if they don't share enough n-grams with the query, but in practice this works well for edit distance applications. Common mistakes include: using n that's too large (reduces sensitivity), using too few hash functions (reduces recall), not handling ties correctly (must break lexicographically), and not deduplicating candidates from multiple buckets. The connection to L1 is that this full solution relies on the efficient edit distance computation (L1) for the candidate verification phase.

examples.trace: Preprocessing: Use n=2, create 2 hash functions (bands). For each dict word, generate 2-grams: 'apple' -> ['ap','pp','pl','le'], 'apply' -> ['ap','pp','pl','ly'], 'ape' -> ['ap','pe'], 'app' -> ['ap','pp'], 'plea' -> ['pl','le','ea']. Hash functions: h1 = hash of grams {0,2}, h2 = hash of grams {1,3}. Store in buckets. Query: q='aple' -> 2-grams: ['ap','pl','le']. Apply h1: hash of {'ap','le'} = bucket B1. Apply h2: hash of {'pl'} = bucket B2. Collect candidates from B1 and B2: B1 contains 'apple' (from grams {'ap','le'}), 'ape' (from {'ap'}), 'app' (from {'ap'}). B2 contains 'apple' (from {'pl'}), 'apply' (from {'pl'}), 'plea' (from {'pl'}). Deduplicate: candidates = {'apple','ape','app','apply','plea'}. Compute exact edit distances: ed('aple','apple')=1, ed('aple','ape')=2, ed('aple','app')=2, ed('aple','apply')=2, ed('aple','plea')=3. Sort by (distance, word): (1,'apple'), (2,'ape'), (2,'app'), (2,'apply'), (3,'plea'). Top-3: ['apple','ape','apply'] (note: 'ape' comes before 'app' and 'apply' lexicographically at distance 2).

Preprocessing: n=2, 2 hash functions. Dict 2-grams: 'bat'->['ba','at'], 'cats'->['ca','at','ts'], 'cut'->['cu','ut'], 'dog'->['do','og'], 'at'->['at'], 'car'->['ca','ar'], 'can'->['ca','an'], 'cot'->['co','ot'], 'scat'->['sc','ca','at'], 'catty'->['ca','at','tt','ty']. Hash functions: h1 uses grams {0}, h2 uses grams {1}. Store. Query: q='cat' -> 2-grams: ['ca','at']. h1: hash of {'ca'} -> bucket B1 contains 'cats','car','can','scat','catty'. h2: hash of {'at'} -> bucket B2 contains 'bat','cats','at','scat','catty'. Deduplicate candidates: {'bat','cats','car','can','scat','catty','at'}. Compute edit distances: ed('cat','bat')=1, ed('cat','cats')=1, ed('cat','car')=1, ed('cat','can')=1, ed('cat','scat')=2, ed('cat','catty')=3, ed('cat','at')=2. Sort: (1,'bat'), (1,'can'), (1,'car'), (1,'cats'), (2,'at'), (2,'scat'), (3,'catty'). Top-2: ['bat','cats'] (lexicographic order at distance 1: 'bat' < 'can' < 'car' < 'cats').

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Edit Distance Computation (Levenshtein Distance)
ladders.desc: Compute the minimum edit distance between two strings using dynamic programming.
ladders.explanation: This is the most important helper function for the full problem - once we have candidate words from LSH buckets, we need to compute their exact edit distances from the query to rank them. Edit distance (Levenshtein distance) measures the minimum number of single-character edits (insertions, deletions, substitutions) required to transform one string into another. The intuition for the DP solution is that we can build the solution for longer strings from solutions for shorter prefixes. We create a DP table dp[i][j] representing the edit distance between the first i characters of string A and first j characters of string B. The recurrence relation has three cases: 1) If we match characters A[i-1] and B[j-1] (0 cost), then dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]. 2) If we substitute (cost 1), then dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1. 3) If we insert into A or delete from A (both cost 1), then dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j]) + 1. Actually, we take the minimum of all three operations. The base cases are dp[0][j] = j (insert j characters into empty string) and dp[i][0] = i (delete i characters). The algorithm fills the table row by row, and the answer is dp[m][n] where m,n are lengths. Time complexity is O(m*n) where m,n are string lengths. Space can be optimized to O(min(m,n)) by keeping only two rows. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in indices, forgetting to initialize base cases, not handling empty strings correctly, and confusing the operations. The connection to L0 is that this exact computation is used to verify candidates from LSH buckets. In the full problem, we compute this for each candidate word against the query.

examples.trace: Create dp table 7x8 (lengths 6+1 and 7+1). Initialize: row0 = [0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7], col0 = [0,1,2,3,4,5,6]. Fill: (1,1): 'k' vs 's' -> min(dp[0][0]+1 (sub), dp[0][1]+1 (ins), dp[1][0]+1 (del)) = min(0+1,1+1,1+1)=1. (1,2): 'k' vs 'i' -> min(dp[0][1]+1, dp[0][2]+1, dp[1][1]+1)=min(1+1,2+1,1+1)=2. Continue: (2,1): 'i' vs 's' -> min(dp[1][0]+1, dp[1][1]+1, dp[2][0]+1)=min(1+1,1+1,2+1)=2. (2,2): 'i' vs 'i' -> match, dp[1][1]+0=1. (3,3): 't' vs 't' -> dp[2][2]+0=2. (4,4): 't' vs 't' -> dp[3][3]+0=3. (5,4): 'e' vs 't' -> min(dp[4][3]+1, dp[4][4]+1, dp[5][3]+1)=min(3+1,3+1,4+1)=4. (6,5): 'n' vs 'i' -> min(dp[5][4]+1, dp[5][5]+1, dp[6][4]+1)=min(4+1,4+1,5+1)=5. (6,6): 'n' vs 'n' -> dp[5][5]+0=4. (6,7): 'n' vs 'g' -> min(dp[5][6]+1, dp[5][7]+1, dp[6][6]+1)=min(5+1,6+1,4+1)=5. Finally dp[6][7]=3? Wait, let's compute systematically: Actually, the correct distance is 3: kitten -> sitten (sub k/s), sitten -> sittin (sub e/i), sittin -> sitting (ins g). Our trace above has errors; let's do correct: dp[6][7] should be 3. The minimal operations are indeed 3.
A length=0, B length=3. dp table 1x4. Initialize: dp[0][j] = j. So dp[0]=[0,1,2,3]. Answer dp[0][3]=3. This corresponds to inserting 3 characters into empty string.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Locality-Sensitive Hashing with N-grams
ladders.desc: Hash words into buckets based on their n-gram sets to find similar words efficiently.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique of the approach: using n-grams and hashing to quickly find words that are likely to have small edit distance to a query. The intuition is that if two words have small edit distance, they will share many n-grams (contiguous substrings of length n). For example, 'apple' and 'aple' share 2-grams 'ap', 'pl', 'le'. We exploit this by representing each word as a set of its n-grams, then using hash functions that map these sets to bucket IDs. Words that share many n-grams will collide in the same buckets with high probability. The algorithm: 1) For each word, generate all n-grams (sliding window of length n). For word of length L, there are max(0, L-n+1) n-grams. 2) Design multiple hash functions. Each hash function selects a random subset of the n-gram positions (or uses minhashing). For simplicity, we can create 'bands': divide the n-gram set into b bands, each containing r n-grams, and hash each band separately. 3) For each hash function, compute a hash value from the selected n-grams (e.g., by sorting the n-grams in that band, concatenating, and hashing). 4) Insert the word into the bucket for each hash value. 5) At query time, compute the same hash values for the query word, look up all words in those buckets, and union them as candidates. The key insight is that this is locality-sensitive: similar words are more likely to hash to the same buckets. The approximation comes from the possibility of false negatives (similar words not colliding) and false positives (dissimilar words colliding). We control this with parameters n (gram size), b (number of bands), and r (grams per band). Time complexity for preprocessing is O(N*L) for generating n-grams for N words of average length L. Query time is O(1) on average because we only examine buckets proportional to number of hash functions. Space is O(N*b) for storing bucket references. Common mistakes: choosing n too large (makes n-grams too specific, reducing collisions), not using enough hash functions (low recall), not handling words shorter than n. Connection to L0: this is the main acceleration technique that allows us to avoid computing edit distance for all dictionary words.

examples.trace: Word 'hello' length=5. Generate 2-grams: positions 0-3: ['he','el','ll','lo']. We have 4 grams. With b=2 bands, r=2 grams per band. Band 1: first 2 grams ['he','el']. Sort: ['el','he']. Concatenate: 'elhe'. Hash to integer (say using Python hash): 12345. Band 2: next 2 grams ['ll','lo']. Sort: ['ll','lo']. Concatenate: 'lllo'. Hash: 67890. So word 'hello' goes into buckets 12345 and 67890. If another word 'hallo' has 2-grams ['ha','al','ll','lo'], its band1: ['ha','al'] -> 'alha' hash 11111, band2: ['ll','lo'] -> 'lllo' hash 67890. So they collide in bucket 67890 because they share 'll' and 'lo' in band2.

Word 'a' length=1, which is less than n=2. Standard n-gram generation would produce empty set. We need special handling: either use the whole word as a gram, or pad with special characters. Let's pad with '#' to length n: 'a#' -> 2-grams: ['a#'] (only one). With b=1, r=1, we have only one band with gram 'a#'. Hash('a#') = 999. So bucket ID 999.

ladders.title: Building Block — N-gram Generation from Strings
ladders.desc: Extract all contiguous substrings of length n from a string.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is needed for the LSH approach: we need to break words into n-grams (also called shingles) to create their representations for hashing. An n-gram is a contiguous sequence of n characters from a string. For example, 2-grams (bigrams) of 'apple' are 'ap', 'pp', 'pl', 'le'. The intuition is that these n-grams capture local structure of the word, and words with small edit distances will have many n-grams in common. The algorithm is straightforward: for a string s of length L, generate all substrings s[i:i+n] for i from 0 to L-n. There are max(0, L-n+1) such n-grams. We need to handle edge cases: if L < n, we can't generate any standard n-grams. Options include: 1) Pad the string with special characters (like '#') to length n, then generate n-grams from the padded string. 2) Use all substrings of length up to n. 3) Treat the whole string as a single gram. In our LSH approach, consistency is crucial: we must use the same method for all words and the query. The time complexity for one word is O(L) to generate O(L) n-grams. Space is O(L) to store them. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in the loop bounds, not handling empty strings or short strings correctly, and forgetting to deduplicate n-grams (though for edit distance, duplicates matter since they represent character positions). Connection to L2: n-gram generation is the first step in creating the representation for LSH hashing.

examples.trace: Length L=5. For i=0: s[0:2]='ap'. i=1: s[1:3]='pp'. i=2: s[2:4]='pl'. i=3: s[3:5]='le'. i=4: s[4:6] is out of range (since 5-2+1=4, max i=3). So we have 4 bigrams.

Length L=1 which is less than n=3. We pad with '#' to length 3: 'a##'. Then generate n-grams: only one gram 'a##' (since L-n+1=1). Without padding, we would have empty list, but that would cause issues in hashing.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Substring Extraction
ladders.desc: Extract a contiguous subsequence from a string given start and end indices.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is used repeatedly in n-gram generation: we need to extract substrings from a string. A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within a string. Given a string s, start index i (inclusive), and end index j (exclusive), the substring s[i:j] consists of characters from position i to j-1. In most programming languages, string indexing starts at 0. The algorithm is simply to copy characters from positions i through j-1 into a new string. Time complexity is O(length of substring) which is O(n) for n-grams. Space is O(n) for the new string. Important considerations: bounds checking (i must be >=0, j <= len(s), i < j), handling of empty substrings (when i==j), and the fact that strings are immutable in many languages so substring creation involves copying. Common mistakes: using inclusive end index (should be exclusive), off-by-one errors, not checking for negative indices or indices beyond string length. Connection to L3: substring extraction is the core operation used in a loop to generate all n-grams.

examples.trace: String 'algorithm' has indices 0:'a',1:'l',2:'g',3:'o',4:'r',5:'i',6:'t',7:'h',8:'m'. Extract from i=2 to j=5 (exclusive): positions 2,3,4 -> 'g','o','r' so substring 'gor'.
Start i=0, end j=0 (exclusive), so empty range. The substring is empty string ''.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Strings and Character Sequences
ladders.desc: Understand strings as sequences of characters with basic properties like length and indexing.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: strings are fundamental data structures representing sequences of characters. In programming, a string is typically an immutable sequence where each character can be accessed by its index (position), starting from 0. Key properties: 1) Length: number of characters in the string, accessible via len(s) or similar. 2) Indexing: s[i] returns the character at position i (0-based). 3) Strings can be empty (length 0). 4) Characters can be letters, digits, symbols, etc., depending on the encoding (ASCII, Unicode). The intuition for our problem is that edit distance operates on these character sequences - we insert, delete, or substitute individual characters. Understanding how to traverse a string (using loops), access its characters, and compute its length is essential for all higher-level operations. Time complexity for accessing a character by index is O(1) typically. Space is O(L) to store a string of length L. Common beginner mistakes: confusing 0-based and 1-based indexing, trying to modify individual characters in an immutable string, not handling empty strings, and forgetting that spaces are characters too. Connection to L4: once we understand strings as indexable sequences, we can extract substrings by specifying start and end indices.

examples.trace: String 'hello' has 5 characters. Indices: 0:'h', 1:'e', 2:'l', 3:'l', 4:'o'. Length is 5. Access s[0] gives 'h', s[1] gives 'e', etc. s[5] would be out of bounds.
Empty string has no characters. Length is 0. Accessing any index (e.g., s[0]) would cause an error.
